mission.title: Install and Configure Dragonfly
mission.description: Dragonfly is a modern in-memory data store that serves as a drop-in replacement for Redis and Memcached, offering significantly higher throughput and lower resource consumption. It is ideal for applications requiring fast data access and caching capabilities. Key trade-offs include compatibility with existing Redis/Memcached applications and the need for Kubernetes for deployment.

steps.title: Add the Dragonfly Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Dragonfly Helm repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install Dragonfly using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add dragonfly https://dragonflydb.github.io/charts

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Dragonfly Helm chart repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Dragonfly CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Dragonfly to function properly in your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dragonflydb/dragonfly/v1.37.0/deploy/crds/dragonflydb_v1alpha1_dragonfly_crd.yaml

```

> This command installs the necessary CRDs.

steps.title: Install Dragonfly using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs the Dragonfly application in a specified namespace using the Helm chart.

```bash

helm install dragonfly dragonfly/dragonfly --namespace dragonfly --create-namespace --version 1.37.0

```

> This command installs Dragonfly in the 'dragonfly' namespace.

steps.title: Create an example Dragonfly custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates an example custom resource for Dragonfly to demonstrate its functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: dragonflydb.io/v1alpha1
kind: Dragonfly
metadata:
  name: example-dragonfly
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

```

> This command creates a Dragonfly instance with 3 replicas.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: dragonflydb.io/v1alpha1
kind: Dragonfly
metadata:
  name: example-dragonfly
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Dragonfly custom resource is ready
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Dragonfly custom resource to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get dragonfly example-dragonfly -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'

```

> This command retrieves the status of the Dragonfly instance.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Dragonfly
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Dragonfly installation from your Kubernetes cluster. Be aware that this will delete all associated data and resources.

```bash

helm uninstall dragonfly --namespace dragonfly

kubectl delete namespace dragonfly

```

> This command uninstalls the Dragonfly release.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Dragonfly
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your data. This step will upgrade Dragonfly to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get dragonfly example-dragonfly -o yaml > backup-dragonfly.yaml

helm upgrade dragonfly dragonfly/dragonfly --namespace dragonfly --version 1.37.0

kubectl get dragonfly example-dragonfly -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'

```

> Backup the current Dragonfly configuration.
> This command upgrades Dragonfly to the specified version.
> Verify that the Dragonfly instance is running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get dragonfly example-dragonfly -o yaml > backup-dragonfly.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Error creating custom resource: ...'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the CRDs are not installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reinstall the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dragonflydb/dragonfly/v1.37.0/deploy/crds/dragonflydb_v1alpha1_dragonfly_crd.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Dragonfly instance is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the pods are not starting due to resource constraints.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with: kubectl logs -l app=dragonfly -n dragonfly

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Dragonfly'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate network issues or service misconfiguration.

**Fix:**
Verify the service is running with: kubectl get svc -n dragonfly

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources to schedule pod'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough resources available.

**Fix:**
Scale up your Kubernetes nodes or adjust the resource requests in the Dragonfly configuration.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new Redis commands, improved memory management, and enhanced metrics collection.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the output 'Running' when checking the status of the Dragonfly instance with: kubectl get dragonfly example-dragonfly -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF

kind: Dragonfly
metadata:
  name: example-dragonfly
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
